content.text: Forecast vs Actual Budget Review Cadence

A single budget set at the start of the year will be wrong by February. Claude usage grows as teams build more features, onboard more users, and discover new use-cases. A forecast-vs-actual review cadence keeps the budget aligned with reality and gives teams enough lead time to optimise spend or request adjustments before guardrails fire.

This document covers four review cadences (weekly, monthly, quarterly, annual), the inputs and outputs of each, the variance analysis that flags teams needing attention, and the forecasting models that project future spend from historical patterns.

The weekly digest is an automated report that gives every team lead a pulse check without requiring a meeting. It covers seven-day spend, week-over-week change, and a month-end projection for any team trending toward guardrail territory.

import anthropic
import pandas as pd
import datetime

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def weekly_spend_digest(ledger_df: pd.DataFrame, week_start: datetime.date) -> dict:
    """
    Compute week-over-week spend by team and flag teams trending toward 90%+ by month end.
    """
    week_end = week_start + datetime.timedelta(days=6)
    prev_start = week_start - datetime.timedelta(days=7)
    prev_end = week_start - datetime.timedelta(days=1)

    def filter_week(df, start, end):
        mask = (
            pd.to_datetime(df["timestamp_utc"]).dt.date >= start
        ) & (
            pd.to_datetime(df["timestamp_utc"]).dt.date <= end
        )
        return df[mask]

    this_week = filter_week(ledger_df, week_start, week_end)
    prev_week = filter_week(ledger_df, prev_start, prev_end)

    this_by_team = this_week.groupby("team")["cost_usd"].sum()
    prev_by_team = prev_week.groupby("team")["cost_usd"].sum()

    rows = []
    for team in set(this_by_team.index) | set(prev_by_team.index):
        this = this_by_team.get(team, 0.0)
        prev = prev_by_team.get(team, 0.0)
        wow_pct = (this - prev) / prev * 100 if prev > 0 else 0.0
        rows.append({"team": team, "this_week_usd": round(this, 2),
                     "prev_week_usd": round(prev, 2), "wow_pct": round(wow_pct, 1)})

    return {
        "week": str(week_start),
        "teams": sorted(rows, key=lambda r: r["this_week_usd"], reverse=True),
        "total_this_week_usd": round(this_week["cost_usd"].sum(), 2),
    }

def project_month_end(
    team: str,
    spend_to_date: float,
    monthly_budget: float,
    days_elapsed: int,
    days_in_month: int,
) -> dict:
    """Linear projection of spend to month-end."""
    daily_rate = spend_to_date / days_elapsed if days_elapsed > 0 else 0
    projected = daily_rate * days_in_month
    utilisation = projected / monthly_budget if monthly_budget > 0 else 0
    return {
        "team": team,
        "projected_month_usd": round(projected, 2),
        "monthly_budget_usd": monthly_budget,
        "projected_utilisation": round(utilisation, 3),
        "alert": utilisation >= 0.90,
    }
The digest is distributed via Slack (#finops-ai) and email every Monday morning. It includes:
Each team's 7-day spend sorted by cost, with WoW change percentage highlighted green (decrease) or red (increase > 10%)
The three use_case / model combinations that generated the most spend across all teams in the past 7 days
For any team tracking toward 90%+ of monthly budget by month-end, show the projection prominently with a link to the exception request form
System-wide cache hit rate for the week; teams with hit rate below 40% are flagged for prompt caching review

The monthly review is the primary governance touchpoint for budget accountability. It happens in the first week after month close and produces a written variance explanation for every team that deviated more than 15% from budget.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def monthly_variance_report(
    actuals: dict,           # {"team": actual_usd}
    budgets: dict,           # {"team": budget_usd}
    prev_month_actuals: dict,
    period: str,
    variance_threshold: float = 0.15,
) -> dict:
    """
    Compute actual vs budget variance and MoM change per team.
    Flag teams with variance exceeding threshold for written explanation.
    """
    rows = []
    for team, budget in budgets.items():
        actual = actuals.get(team, 0.0)
        prev = prev_month_actuals.get(team, 0.0)
        variance_pct = (actual - budget) / budget * 100 if budget > 0 else 0
        mom_pct = (actual - prev) / prev * 100 if prev > 0 else 0
        requires_explanation = abs(variance_pct) > variance_threshold * 100
        rows.append({
            "team": team,
            "actual_usd": round(actual, 2),
            "budget_usd": round(budget, 2),
            "variance_usd": round(actual - budget, 2),
            "variance_pct": round(variance_pct, 1),
            "mom_pct": round(mom_pct, 1),
            "requires_explanation": requires_explanation,
        })

    df = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("variance_pct", ascending=False)
    flagged = df[df["requires_explanation"]]

    return {
        "period": period,
        "total_actual_usd": round(sum(actuals.values()), 2),
        "total_budget_usd": round(sum(budgets.values()), 2),
        "teams": df.to_dict("records"),
        "flagged_teams": flagged["team"].tolist(),
        "explanation_required_by": "5 business days after report distribution",
    }
Teams flagged for variance must submit a written explanation within 5 business days covering:
Root cause of the variance (new feature launch, user growth, model upgrade, operational incident)
Whether the variance is expected to continue (structural) or was a one-time event
Mitigation actions taken or planned if the variance was unintended
Budget adjustment request if the variance reflects a permanent change in usage level

Variance explanations that say only 'increased usage' are not sufficient. The explanation must link the cost change to a specific business event or technical change. Vague explanations get sent back for revision — not accepted.
Return surplus to central pool or reforecast
No immediate change; monitor next month
Budget rebalance review; may require reallocation from other teams
Emergency budget review; immediate cost reduction required

The monthly review updates a rolling 3-month forecast used to project budget sufficiency and detect trends before they become guardrail events. A weighted moving average with a growth assumption is sufficient for most programs; more sophisticated time series methods (SARIMA, Prophet) are warranted only if usage shows strong seasonality.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

def rolling_3month_forecast(
    monthly_actuals: list[dict],  # [{"month": "2025-04", "total_usd": 12400}, ...]
    growth_assumption: float = 0.10,  # 10% MoM growth assumption
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Produce a 3-month forward forecast using weighted moving average
    with a growth multiplier.
    Weights: most recent month = 0.5, two months ago = 0.3, three months ago = 0.2.
    """
    if len(monthly_actuals) < 3:
        raise ValueError("Need at least 3 months of actuals for a weighted forecast.")

    recent = monthly_actuals[-3:]
    weights = np.array([0.2, 0.3, 0.5])
    base = float(np.dot([m["total_usd"] for m in recent], weights))

    forecasts = []
    for i in range(1, 4):
        projected = base * (1 + growth_assumption) ** i
        # Parse last month to get next month string
        last = recent[-1]["month"]
        y, m = int(last[:4]), int(last[5:])
        m += i
        if m > 12:
            m -= 12
            y += 1
        forecasts.append({
            "month": f"{y:04d}-{m:02d}",
            "projected_usd": round(projected, 2),
            "growth_assumption_pct": growth_assumption * 100,
            "method": "weighted_moving_average",
        })

    return forecasts
Recalibrate growth assumption; check for structural shift
Review use-case growth rates by team; update model
Acceptable - 3-month forecast is directional, not precise

The quarterly review is a decision-making meeting with Finance and Engineering leadership. It covers the full quarter's performance, updates the annual budget forecast, and sets optimisation targets for the next quarter.
QoQ spend by team and environment (15 minutes)
Cost efficiency metrics: cost per unit of output by use-case (10 minutes)
Model tier migration opportunities: which use-cases are candidates for downtiering to Haiku? (10 minutes)
Annual budget pace: are we on track to hit annual budget, or does the forecast show an overshoot? (10 minutes)
Rate card and pool rule updates for next quarter (5 minutes)
Optimisation targets and owners for Q+1 (10 minutes)

Raw spend is a misleading signal without normalisation. The quarterly review tracks cost per unit of output to distinguish 'spending more because the product grew' from 'spending more because efficiency declined':

Annual planning happens in Q4 and sets budgets for the following fiscal year. The key inputs are full-year actuals, team growth plans, use-case expansion roadmaps, and any Anthropic pricing signals available.

The most accurate annual budgets are built bottom-up: each team submits a budget request broken down by use-case and environment, based on their growth projections. AI FinOps aggregates these, applies cross-team shared cost and overhead, and produces a consolidated budget proposal.

# Annual budget request template (submitted by each team lead)
team: platform-engineering
fiscal_year: 2026
cost_center: CC-1042

production:
  chat_assistant:
    current_year_actual_usd: 48000
    growth_assumption_pct: 25        # 25% user growth expected
    model_mix: {claude-sonnet-5: 0.7, claude-haiku-4-5-20251001: 0.3}
    optimisation_initiatives:
      - prompt_caching: expected_savings_pct: 20
      - batch_api_migration: expected_savings_pct: 10
    requested_budget_usd: 45000      # lower than projection due to optimisations

  code_review:
    current_year_actual_usd: 22000
    growth_assumption_pct: 40        # new teams onboarding
    model_mix: {claude-opus-4-8: 0.2, claude-sonnet-5: 0.8}
    optimisation_initiatives: []
    requested_budget_usd: 31000

development_and_staging:
  total_requested_usd: 8000          # flat; sandbox budget unchanged

total_requested_usd: 84000
notes: >
  Code review growth reflects 4 new engineering teams onboarding in H1 2026.
  Chat assistant savings assume prompt caching rollout completing by Q1 2026.

The forecast-vs-actual review cadence operates at four time scales: weekly automated digests catch runaway trends before they hit guardrails; monthly variance reviews create accountability for budget deviations with written explanations required; quarterly steering reviews align Finance and Engineering on efficiency targets; and annual planning builds budgets from the ground up using team-specific growth projections. Cost efficiency metrics (cost per unit of output) are the key normalisation signal that distinguishes legitimate growth from degraded efficiency.
